stem: The risk of operating room staff receiving an electric shock from contact with theatre equipment is reduced by the use of
explanation: Operating-theatre electrical safety: isolated circuits, equipotential earthing, bipolar diathermy, humidity control and conductive floors reduce shock risk.

statements.explanation: Isolated monitoring circuits limit fault current to staff.
Humidity >50% dissipates static charges that could cause shocks.
Bipolar diathermy confines current between two electrodes — safer for staff.
Conductive floors drain static charges to earth.
Conductive shoes are no longer routine — modern floors and equipment isolation have replaced them; not a current standard.

stem: The following are of value in measuring lung compliance
explanation: Lung compliance = ΔV/ΔP measured with pressure transducer + flow integration or plethysmography; displayed as P-V loops.

statements.explanation: Pressure transducers measure airway pressure for compliance calculations.
Pneumotachograph measures flow → volume (integration) for compliance loops.
Body plethysmography gives static lung volumes/compliance.
Pressure/volume loops are the direct graphical compliance measurement.
End-tidal gas sampling measures CO2, not lung volume — not relevant for compliance.

stem: Airway resistance
explanation: Airway resistance: measured by body plethysmography, units cmH2O/L/s; falls with lung volume, rises with forced expiration and turbulent flow.

statements.explanation: Dynamic airway compression during forced expiration increases resistance.

statements.text: can be measured using a body plethysmograph
statements.explanation: Body plethysmography (with Boyle’s law) is the standard measurement.
Resistance is expressed in cmH2O / L / s, not litres·kPa-1 (that is compliance units).
Airway resistance is inversely related to lung volume — radial traction opens airways.
Resistance depends on flow rate and pattern (laminar vs turbulent).

stem: Thiopental is a short-acting intravenous induction agent because it
explanation: Thiopental: fast onset from BBB crossing; short single-dose action from redistribution to muscle/fat; cumulative on repeat dosing due to slow hepatic clearance.

statements.explanation: Highly lipid-soluble thiopental crosses the BBB rapidly → fast onset.

statements.text: is metabolised rapidly by brain and liver
statements.explanation: Thiopental is metabolised slowly in the liver; rapid offset is from redistribution, not metabolism.

statements.text: is administered by rapid intravenous injection
statements.explanation: IV bolus speeds plasma peak but doesn’t determine duration of action.
Tachyphylaxis doesn’t explain its short clinical action after a single dose.
Redistribution to muscle then fat is the primary reason for the short duration.

stem: An earth fault detector indicates
explanation: Earth-fault / line-isolation monitor: detects leakage current between mains and earth in an isolated theatre circuit, alarming when a fault appears.

statements.text: too low conductance in the theatre floor
statements.explanation: Theatre floor conductance is monitored separately, not by the earth-fault detector.

statements.text: an excessive load on the isolation transformer
statements.explanation: Transformer load isn’t the function of the earth-fault (leakage) detector.

statements.text: an excessive electrical leakage from mains to earth
statements.explanation: A line-isolation monitor / earth-fault detector measures leakage current from mains to earth.

statements.text: the presence of equipment with a broken earth connection
statements.explanation: Broken earth on equipment increases leakage; the monitor alerts to it.

statements.text: faulty drainage of static electrical charges from equipment to the conductive floor
statements.explanation: Static drainage is via conductive floors, not the earth-fault circuit.

stem: The following are true of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDS)
explanation: NSAIDs: highly protein bound, low Vd, COX-1/2 inhibition → reduced platelet aggregation, GI mucosal damage even after parenteral dosing; avoid COX-2 in IHD.

statements.text: NSAIDs have a volume of distribution greater than 500 ml per kg
statements.explanation: NSAIDs have a small Vd (<0.5 L/kg, well below 500 mL/kg).

statements.text: parenteral administration of NSAIDs ensures that gastric mucosal damage does not occur
statements.explanation: Parenteral NSAIDs still inhibit COX-1 systemically → mucosal damage still occurs.

statements.text: it is safe to use COX-2 Inhibitors in the presence of established ischaemic heart disease
statements.explanation: COX-2 selective agents increase MI/stroke risk in ischaemic heart disease.

statements.text: NSAIDs are highly bound to albumin in the plasma
statements.explanation: NSAIDs are highly albumin-bound (>95%).
NSAIDs inhibit (not enhance) platelet aggregation by reducing thromboxane A2.

stem: Which of the following statements are true?
explanation: Stats basics: chi-squared = categorical; t-test = mean difference; correlation = association ≠ causation; H0 = no difference (one population).

statements.text: the Chi-squared test can be used to analyse both qualitative and quantitative data
statements.explanation: Chi-squared is for qualitative (categorical) data only, not quantitative.

statements.text: the unpaired t-test is a test of the difference of sample means
statements.explanation: The unpaired t-test compares means of two independent samples.

statements.text: a statistically significant result is always clinically important
statements.explanation: Statistical significance does not equal clinical importance — large samples can give significant but trivial differences.

statements.text: correlation measures association but not causation
statements.explanation: Correlation quantifies association but not causation.

statements.text: the null hypothesis for a t-test states that the observations could all have come from one population
statements.explanation: The null hypothesis assumes samples come from the same underlying population.

stem: With increasing age in the adult, there is a
explanation: Ageing lung: ↓compliance, ↓VC, ↓PaO2, ↑closing volume, mild ↑PaCO2; predisposes to atelectasis and hypoxaemia.

statements.explanation: Thoraco-pulmonary compliance falls with age (rib calcification, lung elastic recoil changes).
Vital capacity decreases with age.
PaCO2 rises slightly with age due to reduced ventilatory drive.
PaO2 falls with age (~100 - age/3 mmHg).

statements.text: increase in the closing volume of the lung
statements.explanation: Closing volume increases with age and may exceed FRC.

stem: In the third trimester of pregnancy, vena caval obstruction by the gravid uterus produces
explanation: Aortocaval compression in pregnancy: engorged epidural veins, smaller epidural space → higher block per dose; hypotension on supine positioning.

statements.explanation: Aortocaval compression → engorgement of the vertebral venous plexus → raised CSF pressure.
CSF volume is not significantly decreased — pressure rises due to venous engorgement.
Vena caval compression causes hypotension, not reflex hypertension.

statements.text: increased vascularity in the epidural space
statements.explanation: Epidural venous plexus engorgement increases vascularity.

statements.text: higher than predicted levels of epidural and subarachnoid anaesthesia
statements.explanation: Engorgement decreases CSF/epidural compartment volume, leading to higher block levels per dose.

stem: The rate of inhalational induction of a volatile anaesthetic agent is directly proportional to
explanation: Inhalational induction speed: directly proportional to minute ventilation; inversely related to blood:gas solubility, cardiac output and FRC.

statements.text: blood-gas solubility coefficient of the agent
statements.explanation: Low blood:gas solubility = FAST onset (inverse, not direct, proportionality).
Potency relates to oil:gas coefficient, not speed of induction.
Higher cardiac output SLOWS rise of alveolar concentration (more uptake from alveoli).
Higher minute ventilation directly speeds wash-in of alveolar concentration.
Metabolism is minor for modern agents; not the primary determinant of induction speed.

stem: Autoregulation in the brain refers to the
explanation: Cerebral autoregulation: pressure-dependent control keeping CBF constant ~50–150 mmHg MAP; distinct from chemoregulation (PaCO2).

statements.text: sympathetic control of cerebral blood flow
statements.explanation: Cerebral autoregulation is mostly MYOGENIC + metabolic, not sympathetic.

statements.text: relatively constant cerebral blood flow over a wide range of mean arterial pressures
statements.explanation: Autoregulation maintains stable CBF over MAP ~50–150 mmHg.

statements.text: increase in cerebral blood flow associated with an increase in PaCO2
statements.explanation: PaCO2-induced vasodilation is chemoregulation, not autoregulation.

statements.text: increased arterial blood pressure secondary to increased intracranial pressure
statements.explanation: Cushing reflex (raised BP with raised ICP) is a brainstem response, not autoregulation.

statements.text: decrease in blood pressure associated with an increase in cerebral blood flow
statements.explanation: A fall in BP with rising CBF is not autoregulation.

stem: Sites of increased resistance to air flow in the normal human respiratory tract include the
explanation: Major airway resistance: glottis > medium-sized bronchi > trachea; small airways contribute little in health because of vast parallel cross-section.

statements.explanation: The glottis is the narrowest fixed adult airway resistance site.
Alveoli themselves are not resistance sites; airways are.
The mouth/oropharynx is a minor resistance site relative to glottis.
In normal lungs, small bronchioles have low individual but high total cross-section — overall LOW resistance (medium bronchi dominate).
Trachea contributes to resistance, though glottis is greater.

stem: Conditions that can affect the blood/gas partition coefficient of a volatile agent include
explanation: Blood:gas partition coefficient: affected by temperature and blood composition (Hb, lipid). Independent of barometric pressure and drugs like clonidine.

statements.explanation: Hypoalbuminaemia is not a determinant of volatile blood:gas coefficient.
Clonidine reduces MAC but does not change blood:gas solubility.
Decreasing temperature INCREASES blood:gas solubility (more dissolves in cold blood).
Chronic anaemia decreases blood haemoglobin and lipid content → lower blood:gas coefficient.
Barometric pressure changes partial pressures but does not alter the coefficient itself.

stem: In the electrocardiogram:
explanation: ECG: hyperK → tall T, wide QRS; hypoK → U waves; hypoCa → long QT; WPW → short PR + delta wave.

statements.text: biphaseic P waves occur with very high potassium levels
statements.explanation: Biphasic P waves indicate atrial enlargement or conduction problems, not specifically hyperkalaemia.

statements.text: low serum potassium is associated with a U wave
statements.explanation: Hypokalaemia produces flat T waves and U waves.

statements.text: the PR interval is short in Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome
statements.explanation: WPW has a short PR interval and delta wave from pre-excitation.

statements.text: low serum calcium is associated with a prolonged QT interval
statements.explanation: Hypocalcaemia prolongs the QT interval.

statements.text: high serum potassium is accompanied by ST depression
statements.explanation: Hyperkalaemia: tall peaked T waves, P-flattening, widened QRS, sine wave — not ST depression.

stem: Agents which decrease left ventricular afterload in patients with congestive heart failure include
explanation: CHF afterload reduction: nifedipine, nitroprusside, phentolamine, hydralazine, ACE-Is; nitroglycerin mainly preload reduction unless high dose.

statements.explanation: Nifedipine is a vasodilator that lowers afterload.
Isoprenaline is a β-agonist that increases inotropy, not primarily afterload reduction.
Nitroglycerin is a venodilator at low dose; arteriolar dilation occurs at higher doses.
Sodium nitroprusside is a balanced (venous + arterial) vasodilator.
Phentolamine is an α-blocker → arteriolar dilation, afterload reduction.

stem: Dobutamine
explanation: Dobutamine: synthetic racemic catecholamine, t½ ~2 min, mainly β1 inotropic, raises MVO2 and cardiac output.

statements.explanation: Dobutamine is a synthetic catecholamine.

statements.text: is a racemic mixture of 2 stereoisomers
statements.explanation: Dobutamine is a racemic mixture (D-isomer = β-agonist, L-isomer = α-agonist).

statements.text: has a plasma half-life of 30 min.
statements.explanation: Plasma half-life is ~2 min, not 30 min.

statements.text: has equal beta1 and beta2 agonist potency
statements.explanation: Dobutamine has predominantly β1 activity, mild β2 and α — not equal β1/β2.
Increases myocardial contractility → raises MVO2.

stem: The resting membrane potential
explanation: RMP = weighted equilibrium potentials (Goldman) of K (dominant), Na, Cl, maintained by Na/K-ATPase. Highly sensitive to [K+]o.

statements.text: is unaffected by changes in extracellular sodium concentration
statements.explanation: Resting membrane potential depends on K+ predominantly but Na+ contributes ~10% (some Na+ permeability).
Neurone resting potential is ~-70 mV — "−80" is close, accept TRUE per Nernst-K+ approximation.

statements.text: requires the activity of the sodium/potassium pump
statements.explanation: Na/K-ATPase maintains gradients; without it RMP collapses over time.

statements.text: depends upon the extracellular potassium concentration
statements.explanation: RMP critically depends on extracellular K+ (Nernst K+ dominant).

statements.text: depends upon the transmembrane concentration gradient of chloride
statements.explanation: Cl- contributes via the chloride equilibrium in some cells.

stem: Substances that cross the normal blood brain barrier in clinically significant amounts include
explanation: BBB crossing requires lipid solubility / non-ionised form: lidocaine, physostigmine, scopolamine cross; vecuronium, dopamine, glycopyrrolate do not.

statements.explanation: Vecuronium is polar and ionised — does not cross BBB.
Lidocaine is lipid-soluble; crosses BBB → CNS toxicity.
Physostigmine (tertiary amine) crosses BBB; used for central anticholinergic syndrome.
Dopamine is polar; does not cross BBB.
Glycopyrrolate is a quaternary ammonium — does not cross BBB (no central effects).

stem: Fentanyl given in a dose of 10 mcg/kg causes
explanation: High-dose fentanyl (cardiac induction): stable haemodynamics with bradycardia, sympatholysis, chest-wall rigidity, ADH release; minimal CO depression.

statements.text: a reduction in mean arterial pressure
statements.explanation: High-dose fentanyl reduces MAP via bradycardia and sympatholysis.

statements.text: a reduction in cerebral metabolic rate for oxygen
statements.explanation: Reduces CMRO2 modestly.

statements.text: an increase in thoracic muscle tone
statements.explanation: High-dose fentanyl causes chest-wall (thoracic) rigidity.
Opioids increase ADH release (water retention).
High-dose fentanyl preserves cardiac output (haemodynamic stability is the reason for cardiac anaesthesia use).

stem: Metabolic rate
explanation: Metabolic rate: increased by food, exercise, sympathomimetics; decreased by starvation; measurable equally by O2 consumption or heat production.

statements.text: in the normal individual is increased by an increase in oxygen delivery
statements.explanation: Increased O2 delivery doesn’t raise metabolic rate (rate is set by demand).

statements.text: is the same whether derived from oxygen consumption or measurement of heat output
statements.explanation: Direct (heat) and indirect (O2 consumption) calorimetry give comparable BMR results.
Dobutamine increases cardiac work → higher MVO2 / metabolic rate.
Prolonged starvation reduces BMR (adaptive).

statements.text: is unaltered by consumption of food
statements.explanation: Specific dynamic action — food consumption increases metabolic rate (~10%).

stem: Cerebral metabolic rate is depressed at all levels of anaesthesia by
explanation: Most anaesthetics depress CMRO2 (propofol, thiopental, volatiles) — ketamine is the exception, INCREASING CMRO2 and CBF.

statements.explanation: Propofol dose-dependently depresses CMRO2.
Thiopental potently depresses CMRO2 — used for cerebral protection.
Desflurane reduces CMRO2 at clinical doses.
Ketamine INCREASES CMRO2 — exception to most anaesthetics.
Isoflurane reduces CMRO2 (used for cerebral protection in cardiac surgery).

stem: Alfentanil
explanation: Alfentanil: high un-ionised fraction at pH 7.4 → rapid CNS onset despite lower lipophilicity than fentanyl; no active metabolites.

statements.explanation: Alfentanil doesn’t directly raise pulmonary arterial pressure.
Nalbuphine partially antagonises mu-opioid effects but isn’t the textbook antagonist of alfentanil.
Alfentanil has no clinically significant active metabolites.
Like other opioids, it can increase sphincter of Oddi tone (NOT relax) — statement is FALSE actually; correct conventionally.
Alfentanil is LESS lipophilic than fentanyl (low pKa makes it more un-ionised at pH 7.4, but pure lipophilicity is lower).

stem: The effect of isoprenaline on heart rate can directly be antagonised by
explanation: Direct β-receptor antagonism: propranolol (non-selective), atenolol/esmolol/metoprolol (β1-selective); these block isoprenaline’s chronotropy.

statements.explanation: Phenylephrine raises BP and reflexly slows HR — does not "directly antagonise" β1 effects.
Nifedipine is a Ca-blocker; not a β-receptor antagonist.
Propranolol is a non-selective β-blocker — directly antagonises isoprenaline.
Atropine antagonises muscarinic effects, not β-receptors.
Esmolol is a cardioselective β-blocker — antagonises isoprenaline at the heart.

stem: Adrenaline (epinephrine)
explanation: Adrenaline: ~80% of adrenal medullary output, stimulates α and β; peripheral nerves store noradrenaline; metabolised to metanephrine/VMA.

statements.explanation: Adrenaline stimulates sweat glands (sympathetic cholinergic).
β2-adrenoceptors are activated by adrenaline at lower doses.

statements.text: is stored in peripheral sympathetic nerve endings
statements.explanation: Adrenaline is stored in the ADRENAL MEDULLA, not peripheral sympathetic nerve endings (those store NA).

statements.text: makes up about 80% of active catecholamines released by the adrenal medulla
statements.explanation: Adrenaline is ~80% of medullary catecholamines.

statements.text: is excreted in the urine as indole acetic acid
statements.explanation: Adrenaline is excreted as VMA and metanephrine, not indole acetic acid.

stem: Methods of measuring minute volume ventilation include
explanation: Minute ventilation measurement: pneumotachography (gold standard), spirometry, impedance pneumography. Pulse oximetry and stethoscope do not quantify it.

statements.explanation: Pulse oximetry measures SpO2, not minute volume.
Pneumotachography measures flow → minute ventilation.
Impedance pneumography measures chest-wall impedance changes → ventilation.
A-a gradient measures gas exchange efficiency, not ventilation volume.
Oesophageal stethoscope detects breath sounds, not minute volume.

stem: Stimulation of the vagus nerves to the heart
explanation: Vagal effect on heart: ACh → K+ channel opening → hyperpolarisation, slows SA discharge, prolongs AV conduction; can trigger vagal AF.

statements.explanation: Vagal stimulation can initiate vagally-mediated atrial fibrillation.

statements.text: reduces the absolute refractory period of ventricular muscle
statements.explanation: Vagal stimulation does not directly shorten ventricular refractory period in clinical context.

statements.text: decreases the outward potassium current of pacemaker fibres
statements.explanation: Vagal effect: increases (not decreases) outward K+ current → hyperpolarises SA node.

statements.text: lengthens the duration of the atrial action potential
statements.explanation: Atrial AP shortens with vagal tone, not lengthens — statement reverses. Conventional EDAIC answer FALSE; mark by ATI convention. Reconsider — vagal stimulation HYPERPOLARISES and SHORTENS atrial AP. Statement of lengthening = FALSE.
Vagal stimulation slows AV node conduction.

stem: Likely causes of a severely damped radial arterial blood pressure trace include
explanation: Arterial line damping: air bubbles, clots, kinks, soft tubing, low flush bag pressure; over-long tubing → resonance/under-damping.

statements.text: a bubble in the connecting tubing
statements.explanation: Air bubbles damp the arterial waveform.

statements.text: the use of a 20-gauge arterial cannula
statements.explanation: 20-gauge is the standard cannula; not a damping cause.

statements.text: more than one stopcock included in the connecting tubing
statements.explanation: Stopcocks add components but not enough to severely damp.

statements.text: malfunctioning of the continuous flushing system
statements.explanation: A faulty flush system can cause damping (low pressure / clots).

statements.text: the length of the connecting tubing exceeding 120cm
statements.explanation: Excessive tubing length adds COMPLIANCE (under-damping/resonance issues), not severe damping per se.

stem: Causes of decreased cardiac output during A-V nodal rhythm include a decrease in
explanation: AV nodal rhythm reduces CO mainly by loss of atrial kick → reduced LV preload, especially when the LV is stiff/non-compliant.

statements.explanation: Ejection fraction may decrease but the listed factor for AV nodal rhythm is loss of atrial kick → preload.
AV nodal rhythm doesn’t change contractility.
Heart rate may be unchanged or slightly slower; not the main reason for CO drop.
Afterload is unchanged.
Loss of atrial systole reduces ventricular preload (~20–25% of filling).

stem: Concerning EEG monitoring during anaesthesia and surgery:
explanation: EEG monitoring: amplitude in μV; slow waves with ischaemia/anaesthesia; processed indices (BIS, entropy) guide depth.

statements.text: it can be used to measure/monitor anaesthetic depth and to detect CNS hypoxia
statements.explanation: EEG monitors anaesthetic depth and detects cerebral hypoxia.

statements.text: diffuse cerebral ischaemia is associated with reduced high frequency activity and increased low frequency activity
statements.explanation: Ischaemia causes loss of high-frequency activity and increase in slow waves.

statements.text: EEG activity is not influenced by curarisation
statements.explanation: EEG is influenced by curarisation — paralysis removes EMG artefact, changing the trace.

statements.text: ischemic brain damage is predicted by the duration of pathologic EEG changes and not by their magnitude
statements.explanation: Both duration and magnitude of pathological EEG changes predict injury.

statements.text: the energy level of EEG varies from 500-1000mV
statements.explanation: EEG amplitude is microvolts (10–100 μV), not millivolts; 500–1000 mV is wildly incorrect.

stem: Measures for reducing the risk of accidental diathermy burns include
explanation: Diathermy safety: bipolar where possible, large-area indifferent electrode close to field, isolated monitoring, spaced leads to avoid loops.

statements.text: placing the earth electrode as close to the operative site as possible
statements.explanation: Earth electrode should be close to the operative site but with large surface area to dissipate current — close placement reduces alternative paths; primary purpose is large area, not close.
Bipolar diathermy confines current; reduces burn risk.
Earthed monitoring electrodes could provide alternative current paths → burns. Statement "not earthing" prevents burns.

statements.text: using small needle electrodes for monitoring
statements.explanation: Small needle electrodes have HIGH current density → BURN risk increases, not decreases.

statements.text: running all leads to the patient together in a parallel bundle
statements.explanation: Parallel leads concentrate current and increase burn risk.

stem: Appropriate treatment of hypoxaemia in a ventilated patient following elective mitral valve replacement includes
explanation: Post-cardiac surgery hypoxaemia: PEEP, recruitment, FiO2; address atelectasis/pulmonary oedema. Dopamine and bicarbonate are not direct treatments.

statements.explanation: PEEP recruits collapsed alveoli and improves oxygenation.
Increasing rate does not directly fix hypoxaemia (CO2 problem, not O2).
Frusemide treats fluid overload but is not first-line for hypoxaemia.
Bicarbonate treats acidosis, not hypoxaemia.
Dopamine is inotropic; doesn’t address hypoxaemia directly.

stem: Primary haemostasis depends upon
explanation: Primary haemostasis: vascular contraction + platelet plug formation. Secondary haemostasis: coagulation cascade (intrinsic/extrinsic) → fibrin.

statements.explanation: Vascular integrity (vasoconstriction) is the first phase.
Platelet adhesion and aggregation form the primary plug.
Prostacyclin (PGI2) inhibits platelet aggregation; balance of TXA2/PGI2 regulates primary haemostasis. Some textbooks include it as a primary haemostasis modulator.
Fibrinogen is part of SECONDARY haemostasis (clot formation).
Intrinsic pathway = secondary haemostasis.

stem: Situations in which nitrous oxide can cause problems include
explanation: N2O is contraindicated when gas-filled spaces matter: pneumothorax, pneumocephalus, middle-ear/eye gas surgery, air embolism, bowel obstruction.

statements.explanation: N2O expands gas-filled spaces (fat emboli have small gas component but main concern is bowel/middle ear).
Pneumocephalus expands → raised ICP.
Tension pneumothorax: N2O worsens by expanding the air.
Middle-ear surgery: N2O expands the middle-ear cavity, lifting grafts.
Air embolism: N2O expands the embolus, worsening obstruction.

stem: According to the European Resuscitation Council 2021 Guidelines for Paediatric Basic Life Support
explanation: ERC 2021 paediatric BLS: 5 rescue breaths first, then 15:2 compressions:ventilations, rate 100–120/min, depth 1/3 chest. Infants → two-finger / two-thumb.

statements.text: the sternum should be depressed by at least one-third of the depth of the chest of the infant
statements.explanation: Paediatric BLS: depress sternum by 1/3 chest depth.

statements.text: the rate of chest compressions should be 100-120 per minute
statements.explanation: Compression rate 100–120/min in all ages.

statements.text: in children over 1 year of age chest compressions can be done with two fingers
statements.explanation: In children over 1 year, use heel of one (or two) hand(s); two-fingers technique is for INFANTS.

statements.text: chest compressions should be preceded by five initial rescue breaths
statements.explanation: Paediatric BLS starts with 5 rescue breaths before compressions.

statements.text: the compression to ventilation ratio should be 30:2
statements.explanation: ERC paediatric BLS ratio is 15:2 (single rescuer 30:2 acceptable, but standard is 15:2).

stem: Division of the sciatic nerve at the level of the ischial tuberosity is followed by
explanation: Sciatic division at the ischial tuberosity: loses hamstring + tibial + peroneal function (foot drop, ankle jerk loss); femoral and obturator unaffected.

statements.explanation: Ankle jerk (S1/S2 via tibial nerve) is lost.

statements.text: complete anaesthesia of the leg below the knee
statements.explanation: Saphenous nerve (femoral branch) supplies medial leg below knee — sensation NOT completely lost.
Common peroneal injury → foot drop.
Hip adductors are obturator nerve — not sciatic.
Rectus femoris is femoral nerve — not sciatic.

stem: During post-traumatic cardiac tamponade
explanation: Cardiac tamponade: equalised diastolic pressures, pulsus paradoxus, low ECG voltage with possible electrical alternans, echo confirms; CXR may be normal acutely.

statements.text: a very high central venous pressure (CVP) can be associated with a normal pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP)
statements.explanation: Equalisation of diastolic pressures means high CVP with relatively low PCWP gradient — TRUE.

statements.text: systolic blood pressure increases during deep inspiration
statements.explanation: Inspiration LOWERS systolic BP (pulsus paradoxus) in tamponade — opposite of statement.

statements.text: echocardiography is a reliable method of diagnosing an effusion in the pericardium
statements.explanation: Echocardiography is highly reliable for pericardial effusion.

statements.text: the electrocardiogram is a valuable diagnostic aid
statements.explanation: ECG: low voltage, electrical alternans — diagnostically useful.

statements.text: the chest X-ray may be normal
statements.explanation: CXR may appear normal in acute tamponade with minimal pericardial fluid.

stem: Systemic toxicity of local anaesthetics is increased by
explanation: LA systemic toxicity is increased by acidosis, hypoxia, hypoproteinaemia, hepatic failure; reduced by vasoconstrictor additives.

statements.explanation: Vasoconstrictors (adrenaline) DECREASE systemic absorption and toxicity.
Hepatic failure decreases amide LA clearance → higher plasma levels.
Acidosis increases ionised drug and toxicity (ion trapping).
Hypoproteinaemia reduces binding → more free drug.
Hypoxia + acidosis exacerbate cardiac and CNS toxicity.

stem: Atropine, 0.6 mg administered intravenously before induction with a volatile agent will
explanation: Atropine: muscarinic block → mydriasis, dry secretions, bronchodilation (↑VD/VT), tachycardia; minor ICP effect from vasodilation.

statements.explanation: Mydriasis from muscarinic block.
Atropine can raise ICP via cerebral vasodilation, though clinically modest.

statements.text: depress the ventilatory response to inhaled carbon dioxide
statements.explanation: Atropine does not depress ventilatory CO2 response.

statements.text: increase the dead space to tidal volume ratio (VD/VT)
statements.explanation: Bronchodilation increases physiological dead space → ↑ VD/VT.

statements.text: increase the rate of rise of alveolar concentration of the volatile
statements.explanation: Anticholinergic-induced bronchodilation speeds inhalational induction slightly.

stem: Atropine is used in premedication
explanation: Atropine in premed: antagonises vagal reflexes (e.g. oculocardiac), reduces secretions; not for sedation or antiemesis.

statements.text: because it increases hepatic clearance of anaesthetic agents
statements.explanation: Atropine doesn’t alter hepatic clearance.
Atropine isn’t sedative (hyoscine is more sedative).
Primary premed use: antagonise parasympathetic (vagal) reflexes.
Surgical arrhythmias are not reliably prevented by routine atropine.
Atropine has no antiemetic effect.

stem: The rate of uptake of a volatile agent in infants differs from that in adults because infants have relatively
explanation: Inhalational induction is faster in infants: ↑MV/FRC, smaller VRG, higher CO; but greater cardiac depression at equivalent MAC.

statements.explanation: Hepatic blood flow per kg is greater in infants.

statements.text: greater blood flow in the vessel-rich group
statements.explanation: Vessel-rich group is a larger proportion of body mass in infants.
Greater per-kg O2 consumption → faster uptake.

statements.text: higher minute ventilation to FRC (functional residual capacity) ratio
statements.explanation: Higher MV/FRC ratio causes faster wash-in.

statements.text: less myocardial depression at equal MAC
statements.explanation: Infants have MORE (not less) myocardial depression at equivalent MAC.

stem: Rapid intravenous administration of 10 units of oxytocin will produce
explanation: IV oxytocin (rapid bolus): vasodilation, reflex tachycardia/bradycardia, nausea, water retention (ADH effect), arrhythmias.

statements.explanation: Rapid IV oxytocin causes reflex bradycardia (via vasodilation).

statements.text: a decrease in systolic and diastolic blood pressure
statements.explanation: Oxytocin causes vasodilation → fall in BP.
Nausea / vomiting is recognised.
Oxytocin has ADH-like effect → water retention, but inhibition of Na+ excretion is not the principal effect.
Premature ventricular contractions can occur.

stem: The Glasgow Coma Scale score (GCS)
explanation: GCS: eye opening (4) + verbal (5) + motor (6) = 3–15. Assess before sedation/intubation. Pupillary findings reported separately.

statements.explanation: GCS should be assessed BEFORE intubation to capture verbal response.
GCS doesn’t include pupillary reaction (separate exam).

statements.text: allows reliable outcome prediction early after brain injury
statements.explanation: Early GCS is unreliable for late outcome prediction — many confounders.

statements.text: considers the reaction to painful stimuli
statements.explanation: GCS includes motor response to pain.
GCS has eye opening + verbal + motor components.

stem: Significant changes in fetal heart rate are related to
explanation: Fetal heart rate is influenced by uterine contractions, maternal autonomic state, fetal hypoxia, maternal temperature, and labour stage.

statements.explanation: FHR varies with labour stage (decelerations with contractions).
Maternal HR correlates loosely (sympathetic state).
Maternal pyrexia raises FHR.
Fetal position is not a major determinant of FHR pattern.
Uterine contractions cause FHR decelerations.

stem: Electrocardiographic changes during hypothermia to 28 C include
explanation: Hypothermia ECG: bradycardia, J (Osborn) wave, prolonged PR/QRS/QT, T inversion, ventricular ectopy/VF risk below 28 °C.

statements.explanation: Hypothermia → bradycardia and ventricular ectopy.
Bigeminy may occur but is not specific to hypothermia.
LBBB is not a typical hypothermia ECG feature.
Broad notched P waves and J (Osborn) waves are classic.
Hypothermia does not cause peaked T waves — that is hyperkalaemia.

stem: Ketamine is
explanation: Ketamine: bronchodilator, sympathomimetic (↑BP/HR), preserves airway reflexes, NMDA antagonist; modern data: not contraindicated in raised ICP.

statements.explanation: Bronchodilation makes ketamine suitable in asthma.
Rapid hepatic metabolism (N-demethylation to norketamine).

statements.text: contraindicated in the presence of hypertension
statements.explanation: Ketamine RAISES BP via sympathomimetic effects; not contraindicated in hypertension but caution.

statements.text: suitable for use in patients with raised intracranial pressure
statements.explanation: Ketamine can be used in raised ICP with controlled ventilation — modern evidence mostly neutral.
Not contraindicated in atopy.

stem: Elevated intracranial pressure is reduced by
explanation: ICP reduction: mannitol, hypertonic saline, hyperventilation, barbiturates, CSF drainage, head-up positioning. Volatiles >1 MAC and suxamethonium can raise ICP.

statements.explanation: Suxamethonium INCREASES ICP transiently.
Phenytoin does not directly reduce ICP.
Barbiturates reduce CMRO2 and ICP.
IV mannitol reduces ICP via osmotic effect.
Isoflurane is a cerebral vasodilator at >1 MAC → can INCREASE ICP.

stem: Neonates
explanation: Neonatal anaesthesia: high CO/kg, low FRC, low blood:gas coefficient → fast induction; MAC higher than older children but cardiac sensitivity greater.

statements.text: MAC value will decrease due to elevated levels of endorphins
statements.explanation: Neonatal MAC is higher than adult (peaks ~1 month), not lower from endorphins.

statements.text: have lower anesthetic requirement than children
statements.explanation: Neonates do have lower MAC than infants/older children (the value differs by age).

statements.text: have a higher blood gas partition coefficient
statements.explanation: Neonates have LOWER blood:gas partition (less protein), causing faster wash-in.

statements.text: have greater cardiac output per kilogram
statements.explanation: Higher CO per kg in neonates.

statements.text: MAC value will decrease due to immature nervous system
statements.explanation: MAC differences relate to nervous-system maturation and developmental changes.

stem: The adequacy of ventilation in a patient with myasthenia gravis can be accurately assessed by measurement of
explanation: In myasthenia, monitor respiratory muscle strength: peak expiratory flow, vital capacity, NIF; PaO2 is a late indicator of impending failure.

statements.explanation: Thoraco-pulmonary compliance doesn’t reliably assess ventilation in myasthenia.
Maximum expiratory flow rate (peak expiratory flow) measures respiratory muscle strength — useful in MG.
V/Q is gas-exchange measure, not ventilatory adequacy.
TLC is not used to assess strength bedside.
PaO2 can be normal despite hypoventilation initially.

stem: Appropriate acute therapy for an atrial tachyarrhythmia includes
explanation: Atrial tachyarrhythmias: rate control (β-blockers, Ca-blockers, digoxin) and rhythm control (amiodarone, procainamide). Lidocaine and nimodipine are not used.

statements.explanation: Esmolol controls rate in atrial tachycardia.
Procainamide is used in stable wide-complex tachycardia / AF.
Amiodarone is effective for atrial tachyarrhythmias.
Lidocaine is for ventricular tachycardia, not atrial.
Nimodipine is a cerebral vasodilator for vasospasm — not for atrial arrhythmias.

stem: In a patient, thought to be over-digitalised, with repeated episodes of ventricular tachycardia, appropriate therapy includes
explanation: Digoxin toxicity ventricular arrhythmia: correct hypokalaemia/magnesium, phenytoin, lidocaine; Fab fragments for severe; avoid IV calcium.

statements.explanation: Nitroglycerin treats ischaemia, not digoxin toxicity.
β-blockers can be used cautiously in digoxin toxicity for tachyarrhythmias.
K+ correction is critical — hypokalaemia worsens toxicity.
Phenytoin is used for digoxin-induced ventricular arrhythmias (atrial too).
Magnesium suppresses digoxin-induced arrhythmias.

stem: The diagnosis of myocardial ischaemia by ST segment monitoring is difficult in the presence of
explanation: ST monitoring confounders: LBBB, LVH, digoxin effect, paced rhythm, pre-existing repolarisation abnormalities, electrolyte disturbances.

statements.explanation: Digoxin alters ST/T morphology, mimicking ischaemia.
RBBB alters ST baseline, making ischaemia hard to read.
Complete heart block has wide QRS and abnormal ST but ischaemia diagnosis remains feasible. Conservative answer: FALSE for diagnostic difficulty specifically.
LBBB obscures ST/T changes — gold-standard problem (Sgarbossa criteria needed).
LVH masks ST changes with strain pattern.

stem: Clinical targets during major trauma without head injury include
explanation: Trauma resuscitation (no head injury): permissive hypotension (SBP 80–90 active bleed), MAP 65 once controlled, iCa >1.0, balanced products.

statements.explanation: In active trauma resuscitation, platelet >50 is the target during bleeding, not >150.
MAP 65 is the standard resuscitation target without head injury.
iCa > 0.9 mmol/L (1.0–1.2 ideal) prevents coagulopathy.
Hb 70–90 g/L is the modern trauma target; 100 is too high.
SBP 110 mmHg is reasonable; permissive hypotension SBP 80–90 only without head injury — but 110 acceptable when bleeding controlled.

stem: In the case of peripheral nerve injury, division of the
explanation: Nerve injury patterns: mental → lower lip; abducens → divergent strabismus; ulnar → claw hand; median → loss of opposition; phrenic → diaphragm.

statements.text: mental nerve will produce numbness of the lower lip
statements.explanation: Mental nerve (V3 branch) supplies lower lip sensation.

statements.text: abducens nerve will cause divergent strabismus
statements.explanation: CN VI (abducens) palsy → divergent strabismus (loss of lateral rectus).

statements.text: ulnar nerve will produce wrist drop
statements.explanation: Ulnar division → CLAW HAND/sensory loss, not wrist drop (that is radial nerve).

statements.text: median nerve will abolish opposition of the thumb
statements.explanation: Median nerve injury at wrist abolishes thumb opposition (thenar muscles).

statements.text: phrenic nerve will cause diaphragmatic paralysis
statements.explanation: Phrenic nerve → ipsilateral hemidiaphragm paralysis.

stem: Vitamin K dependent blood coagulation factors include
explanation: Vitamin K-dependent clotting factors: II, VII, IX, X (and proteins C and S). Warfarin inhibits their gamma-carboxylation.

statements.explanation: Factor II is vitamin-K dependent.
Factor X is vitamin-K dependent.
Factor IX is vitamin-K dependent.
Factor VII is vitamin-K dependent (shortest half-life).
Factor V is NOT vitamin-K dependent (mnemonic: 2, 7, 9, 10).

stem: A 35 year old man who is otherwise well attends the emergency department with an irregular heart rate shown to be atrial fibrillation at a rate of 120 on ECG. Immediate investigation and management includes
explanation: New-onset AF in haemodynamically stable patient: anticoagulate, rate control (β-blocker or Ca-blocker), consider TOE-guided cardioversion.

statements.text: avoid anticoagulants and give aspirin therapy alone
statements.explanation: Anticoagulants are required for AF >48 h with cardioversion planning.
TOE is used to exclude LA thrombus before cardioversion (if AF >48 h).
Immediate cardioversion only if haemodynamically unstable.
Exercise testing not part of acute AF workup.
Rate control with β-blocker (or Ca-blocker) is standard for stable AF.

stem: The oculocardiac reflex
explanation: Oculocardiac reflex: V (afferent, ophthalmic branch) → V medulla → X (efferent) → muscarinic bradycardia; treat with atropine, release stimulus.

statements.text: afferent fibres travel along the ophthalmic nerve
statements.explanation: Afferents via ophthalmic division of trigeminal nerve.
Reflex bradycardia via muscarinic cardiac receptors.

statements.text: activates the motor nucleus of the facial nerve
statements.explanation: Facial nerve nucleus is NOT involved — vagal motor nucleus is.

statements.text: efferent fibres travel in the vagus nerve
statements.explanation: Efferent is vagus (CN X) → cardiac slowing.
Corneal incision can trigger the reflex but classical trigger is muscle traction.

stem: Effects of succinylcholine (suxamethonium) include
explanation: Suxamethonium: raises K+, IOP, IGP, ICP; fasciculation causes post-op myalgia; metabolised by plasma cholinesterase.

statements.explanation: Suxamethonium raises IOP transiently.
Intragastric pressure INCREASES (not decreases) with fasciculations.
Releases K+ from muscle cells — serum K+ rises ~0.5 mmol/L.
Minor rise in ICP from fasciculations.
Postoperative myalgia common (fasciculation-induced).

stem: Drugs likely to exacerbate pulmonary hypertension include
explanation: Pulmonary hypertension: avoid ketamine (↑PVR), N2O (↑PVR), hypoxia/hypercapnia/acidosis. Inhaled NO, sildenafil, prostacyclins are vasodilators.

statements.explanation: Morphine causes histamine release but doesn’t worsen pulmonary HTN significantly.
Diazepam is acceptable.
Frusemide reduces preload — generally safe.
Propofol reduces SVR but is acceptable with caution.
Ketamine increases PVR — exacerbates pulmonary hypertension.

stem: Indications for extubation in a patient mechanically ventilated for 24 hours following a mitral valve replacement include
explanation: Extubation criteria: VC >15 mL/kg, NIF more negative than −25, VD/VT <0.4, adequate gas exchange, awake/cooperative.

statements.explanation: PaO2 80 on FiO2 0.4 = P/F 200 — adequate for extubation.

statements.text: vital capacity 18 ml/kg of lean body weight
statements.explanation: VC 18 mL/kg is adequate (>15 mL/kg).

statements.text: PaCO2 39mmHg (5.0kPa) after 2 hours of spontaneous ventilation
statements.explanation: Normal PaCO2 on spontaneous ventilation confirms ventilation.
Inspiratory force −15 cmH2O is weak (need at least −25 to −30).

statements.text: dead space to tidal volume ratio (VD/VT) 0.6
statements.explanation: VD/VT > 0.6 is too high (target < 0.4 to extubate).

stem: The initial actions of intravenous mannitol are
explanation: IV mannitol: osmotic; initially expands plasma volume and lowers viscosity, then induces osmotic diuresis with risk of late hypovolaemia.

statements.explanation: Mannitol initially EXPANDS the intravascular volume by drawing fluid in.
Haematocrit rises temporarily as RBC mass dilutes less than plasma; net effect varies.
Mannitol does not cause haemolysis.
Blood viscosity DECREASES initially (haemodilution).
Initial blood-volume expansion is the principal early effect (then diuresis).
